title: Jordan Taylor's Second Lesson
preview: TT wo years later Rick and I were finally ready to open our business. I still thought about Fawn from time to time and wondered how’s he was doing. In hindsight, I definitely wasn’t prepared for a relationship. Hopefully, I had grown up a little...
body:
TT

wo years later Rick and I were finally ready to open our business. I still thought about Fawn from time to time and wondered how’s he was doing. In hindsight, I definitely wasn’t prepared for a relationship. Hopefully, I had grown up a little more after Fawn or so I thought. There were still some serious lessons I had to learn out here. This time, the woman I chose caused me and my family some major embarrassment, heartache, and pain.
One thing still concerned me about Risk's associates. Just because he was friends with them didn't necessarily mean this was the best partners for us. It seemed like all the facts of this business were not on the table. There was something that was being hidden. No specific signs just subtle ones like vague answers. When I would ask certain questions, they would skirt around them, avoid, dismiss tell me we can discuss it later. I took it as being secretive and dishonest. Rick told me that I was being petty and over thinking things. These guys are seasoned and know what they’re doing. That shouldn’t matter. My questions should have been answered no matter whether they were relevant or not.
My suggestion was to include an exit agreement. This is similar to a prenup for businesses and specifies how either party can dissolve the partnership if things don’t work out. All the members finally convinced me that everything was in order. The documents were signed but in the back of my head I was still uncertain. Rick, and I were now under the umbrella of Trader’s International. We began spending time researching the market, attending trade shows and export import seminars. Eventually, we traveled to establish relationships with the Malaysia buyers we would be working with. There were documents and certificates to be agreed upon and signed to get our business going.
We did a lot of traveling since we were the new kids on the block. We were required to visit the other offices to learn more about the exporting business that we didn’t learn in school. They were stationed in New York, LA, Florida, and Texas. It was always a good time on those trips because Trader’s International knew how to receive and welcome the new kids on the block. There were meetings during the day but, during the evenings it was party time.
California was the biggest base, so we were there quite frequently. I always noticed this Asian woman that worked in the office. She seemed to be shy and looked as if she were nervous or afraid about something. When I asked about who she was they told me that her uncle works for the company and brought her to America from Malaysia. He held one of the top positions in the company. Each time we would see each other in the office smiles were exchanged. The next time I saw her I was going to make sure I asked her name. I had a few minutes before my next meeting, so I was able to have a quick conversation with her. Her name was Hana. Nice to meet you I’m Jordan. Surprisingly, she already knew that.
As I looked at her, she reminded me of my uncle who married an Asian girl from Thailand. All the family laughed at him and ridiculed him for finding a wife through a mail order service. Didn’t know there was any such a thing. It cost him about $10,000.00 to bring her to America. There was a big catch that he wasn’t aware of. Her family was part of the marriage agreement, and they came with the package. Somehow, he didn’t read the provisions of the agreement closely. He was just happy that he was going to get this woman that was a devout Buddhist that he was seeking. We never understood why he couldn’t find one in America.
After my meeting I was able to speak to Hana again. I wanted to know why she never came to any of the after-work parties. Again, I saw this frightful look on her face like someone was watching us while we spoke. She was noticeably nervous. She explained that she was not allowed to be there. Only the people who were invited. I didn’t understand why she couldn’t be there. After all, her uncle was one of the biggest stake holders in the company. It just didn’t make sense. I asked Rick why Hana was not allowed to attend the gatherings after work. He just shrugged his shoulders and tried to change the subject. He definitely knew more than what he was saying, and I was going to find out what it was.
Traveling to Malaysia was quite an experience. Hana’s uncle Sabit, traveled with us for our first time to ensure that all the documents were in order and the deal was successfully made. It always helps to have someone who has done this time and time again. We were excited that we sealed our first deal and there would be many more to come. This was a time to celebrate. We made plans for later that evening for the time and place. Sabit never showed up and Rick didn’t seem too concerned he probably just wanted to hang with some friends instead.
When we got back to L.A. I was going to see if Hana would go out with me. She wasn’t the most beautiful girl in the world, but she was pretty and had a sweet quiet disposition. It’s too bad that many of them feel that in order to be beautiful according to the American standards they have to resort getting eyelid surgery to improve their physical appearance giving them a larger and more symmetric almond-shaped eye. Also, for years they have been hypersexualized and viewed as sexually desirable, exotic, seductive, subservient passive objects. This has played a large part in the violence they have endured. I didn’t care about her eyes or that she was perceived as a sexual object. I was attracted to her and wanted to know her better.
We were scheduled to head back to the main base the next day. That morning there was still no response from Sabit, and we had to go or we would miss our flight. We asked the front desk if he had left us any messages. We were told he had checked out last evening. I thought that was strange, but Rick assured me again that he was probably with friends and that he would meet us at the airport. When we arrived at the gate there was no sign of him. They announced the final call for boarding. The doors closed and we would soon be back in L.A.
We returned to the office thinking maybe Sabit came back early and could do what he wanted. He didn’t have to report to us. He was a big boss, and we reported to him not the other way around. I ran across Hana and asked her on the side did she know where her uncle was. Again, there was that nervous look. She hadn’t seen him either. It’s strange because every time we went to Malaysia. Sabit always returned to the states alone.
While talking to her, I suggested that we go out sometime and get to know each other. She hesitated as if in deep thought but agreed to us hanging out. Now, we all know the rules of dating in the workplace. It is frowned upon, and it is considered unprofessional. It tends to create unnecessary drama and distractions. Here I go taking risks and probably not using my best decision-making skills again or making good choices. Time would tell.
We both liked each other so what the hell. It was agreed that our dating would be kept between us. No one needed to be in our business. Not only that, but Hana was concerned that her uncle might not be too happy with us seeing each other. He was really against dating on the job and wanted her to date only Asian men. We enjoyed the times we spent together visiting art fairs, movies, and good places to eat.
Hana told me she had never dated a Black man before and was curious about our culture and specifically our sexual prowess she had heard about. I was curious about her culture and had never dated an Asian woman. I wondered whether or not Asian women were sexually submissive. See how stereotypes flourish? Well, let’s just say neither of us was disappointed. Nuf said.
The business was really taking off. We were making money and enjoying the lifestyle we worked for. Since we spent most of our time in California, Rick and I decided to move there and get our own apartments. I was happy because I would see more of Hana. There was only one problem. Several months later Sabit introduced her to an Asian man he knew from Malaysia in hopes there would be a future marriage.
Decisions and choices were to be made again. They just kept showing up in my life. Either we were going to end our relationship, or Hana was going to have to come clean about us to her uncle.
If you guessed that our relationship had to come to an end. You were correct. There was nothing else to do. If Hana refused to meet with her uncle’s choice, he was going to send her back to Malaysia. He brought her to America, and he could send her back with family. She didn’t want to leave California. If she was forced to go, we would never see each other again. I had already violated the dating policy in the workplace. If we were caught, I would have been terminated. My hard work to make this business a success would have all been in vain.
Hana went on to build a relationship with her future husband Kenji. She expressed to me that there was no attraction there, he was much older, and she was very unhappy. I didn’t know what to tell her. Sabit already contacted her parents, and they were aware of the possible marriage that would eventually take place.
Rick and I continued to thrive in our business. Even though we were almost old pros, Sabit continued to travel with us for the extra support or so we thought. He never returned with us. I kept my mouth shut and decided the best thing was not to ask too many questions. The office was busy all the time and I was just glad to be working with one of the largest International Companies importing and exporting abroad. To think that we were part of this was a dream come true. Although sometimes a dream can be nightmare too.
Whenever I would get back to the office, I would check on Hana to see how she was doing. Everyone thought we were just good friends and nothing more. We could still talk from time to time. It’s surprising that within those couple of months of dating her I was able to keep it from Rick. We didn’t hang out like we used to, so he wasn’t in my business as much. He seemed preoccupied anyway most of the time. I tried to find out what was going on, but he would always tell me everything was cool. I didn’t think any more about it. Something wasn’t right.
There was no sign of Hana when I returned to the office. No one had seen her in a couple of days. I was concerned and called her with no response. Later that evening there was a knock at my door. It was Hana. The first thing I noticed was how scared she was. When she told me what was going on it was hard to believe. It was bad really bad. She was coming to warn me that our company was about to be shut down indefinitely. It would be closed until further investigation was completed. This meant that everybody from the top executives on down could be fined or subject to serious jail time. Hana was almost hysterical trying to get the right words out, but she wasn’t making any sense. I grabbed and held her tight in hopes that would make her calm down and tell me what was going on. It seemed as though Kenji, Hana’s boyfriend from Malaysia had been tracking Trader’s International for almost a year along with other agents. Little did Sabit know that the man he thought was a friend was working undercover for the Government as an informant. Hana, “Please give me the details.” “Jordan, the company has been sex trafficking young women from Malaysia to work in massage parlors in the cities where we have offices”. Kenji wanted Hana to know so she could start seeking legal counsel. He told her she would probably get a subpoena to testify in court.
Now, I know why Sabit never came back with us. He was busy snatching up prostitutes to work and provide full service or what’s called “A happy ending.” IMB’S or (illicit massage businesses) use the cover of a legitimate massage business to provide commercial sex acts to buying customers. I discovered there are approximately 9,0000 of these businesses with earnings totaling nearly $2.5 billion a year. Guess my suspicions were warranted. My gut told me something was dishonest about this company. Rick has been acting so strange I wonder how much he knew about this. I was pissed that Hana might have known all along of what was going on and she didn’t tell me until after the fact.
Hana was still with me when I called Rick. There was no answer, so we went to his apartment. He wasn’t there. We went back to my place to try and figure out what needed to be done. Hana already told me that Kenji recommend she get an attorney, and I was thinking the same thing too. No matter what Hana knew or didn’t know, it was great that Kenji gave her the heads up so she could be ready for whatever was ahead. We thought to turn on the TV to see if there was any news. Hana screamed. She saw her uncle Sabit and others being walked out of the building. No wonder Rick wasn’t at his apartment. He was being arrested too.
The next day I called the Bar Association to find an Attorney willing to accept me as a client. This was big news in the media. Everyday there was more information about all the team members in every office (New York, Florida, Texas) and what their role was contributing to these illegal massage parlors. This was not going to be some little slap on the wrist crime. This was Global organized crime that the United States and Malaysian Government were going to take down. What a scandal this was.
My mom called me in tears when she saw my picture on the news. She told me I had shamed the family and that all the people in our town were talking about it. To her I was already convicted and sent to jail. At this time, I needed support not condemnation. I think she was just angry that I hadn’t used better judgement. My dad got on the phone and asked how I was handling it. I told him I had an Attorney and assured him that I would be ok. My sisters and brother contacted me to say they were with me and to let them know if I needed anything.
What a nightmare! Remember I said that about dreams. There were endless meetings with my Attorney. Questioning and depositions over and over. What did I know? What was my export business? How were deals made? Where are copies of every contract? How did I spend my time when I was in Malaysia? What were my connections to the top executives? What information did they ever share with me? These were all questions the Attorney said would come out at the trial. I was told not to discuss any information of my case with anyone at Traders’ International. I was told to be very careful because my every move and phone call would be monitored.
Hana and I did continue to talk either at my apartment or sometimes hers. She kept me updated on any new information that might affect us which wasn’t much. She had an inside track with Kenji. Although, he didn’t share anything that would keep us from testifying in court. His job was to gather information, not give it. Rick finally surfaced and was released on bail. He explained that he had his suspicions about what was going on.
Rumors had been whispered around but he didn’t want to get involved or do any snooping. You just hoped that none of it was true. He was actually innocent of any wrongdoing. He didn’t want anything to interfere with our successful business. So, he just ignored the gossip. That’s why he was acting so strange. He didn’t want me to be concerned or bothered. But, at this point we both should have been concerned. Rick wished that he had listened to my suspicions.
Months and months went by as the Prosecution and the Defense prepared their cases. It was a long and difficult road ahead. Found out that Hana knew more than what she claimed to know. She was hanging around with me to see if I was holding back any information that could be reported to Kenji and used in court. She was only interested in saving herself.
Found out that Sabit was well aware of Hana and I seeing each other. He had been monitoring and following us the entire time. Since we were all in Malaysia at the same time. Is intention, was to tie Rick and I to his crimes as well. When you’re caught and trapped you want everybody to go down. Hana’s silence about what she suspected about the trafficking basically through us under the bus.
Sabit testified that Rick and I were just as much a part of the trafficking. “I promise to tell the whole truth nothing but the truth, so help me God.” It doesn’t mean a damn thing. The justice Department thinks because God is added to a stupid sentence that it will make people tell the truth. Putting your hand on a bible. What a joke. It looked pretty bad for us since we were in Malaysia frequently and we were in business with Trader’s International. I guess when Hana found out the axe was coming down, she panicked. If only she had shared this information with us the partnership could have been dissolved through the exit agreement. We would have not been in this mess.
It was difficult sitting through the trial day after day not knowing what the outcome would be. I was thankful that after court we were not escorted by the bailiffs back to a cold jail cell. At least I could go home every night. We paid the price though. Bail was not cheap. Testimony after testimony went on for months. My family did come back and forth to support me during my incarceration. I know it was difficult for them to see me going through this. It was difficult for them. And, hardest on my mom because she still saw me as her baby. Guess she would never get past that I was an adult now and had to take responsibility for my choices and accept the consequences good or bad.
Finally, the Prosecution and Defense gave their closing remarks and now it was time for the judge to rule. From all the evidence and the closing remarks it was determined who the top guilty ones were. They had conspired against two governments to open numerous IMB’S (illicit massage businesses). There were so many charges. The biggest one was transporting prostitutes across International Lines to another Country. The judge adjourned court until tomorrow when sentences would be announced.
Rick and I were nervous because we didn’t want any judgements against us not even probation. Our Attorneys were pretty certain that we would not be charged with any crime. They had investigated our trips going and coming while we conducted our business in Malaysia. They couldn’t find anything that could be held against us. The next day we came prepared for whatever the Judge was going to hand down. The press was overflowing at the court building waiting for the sentences to be announced. One by one names were called and sentences were given. Sabit got the most time of 20 years. All those involved were given stiff sentences and fined as well. It seemed like forever to call the list of names from all the company offices. Fortunately, everyone that worked for the organization were not implemented in the scandal. Rick and I watched as each one received their time and we waited patiently to hear ours.
It had come down to those last few people in Trader’s International who had been charged. It took several days for the Judge to pass sentences. Finally, we were asked to stand for sentencing. Both our parents were there for us and were confident that we had not committed any crime. It least that was their hope. The judge looked at both of us and started reading from his notes. He called our names and told us according to evidence that had been presented that our case was dismissed, and we were free to go.
Of course, all the massage parlors were dismantled and closed down. There were hundreds of them in each city where our offices were located. The young women were not prosecuted since they were brought here to work illegally. They were returned to Malaysia where they went back to the life they knew best. The owner that ran the parlors were all prosecuted as well. No one went unpunished.
It was over for us. And now we had to put this behind us and figure out how to pick up the pieces from the business we had worked so hard to put together. We were able legally to dissolve our partnership from Trader’s International. We were now independent under our business’s original name Midland Soybeans International. We reestablished relationships with those companies in good faith to continue our business deals.
Rick and I decided to stay in California for the time being since everything was already in place there. Our company continued to be successful, and we knew it was important to give back. We decided to add an intern program to the business. We didn’t want anyone else interested in the export business to experience what we did. Life was good for us, and we were thankful.
Oh, you want to know what happened to Hana. She got off with probation. She did try to contact me numerous times. She even came to my apartment to apologize for putting me through all this hell. That chapter of my life was closed, and we never spoke again. And, as far as women are concerned right now, I’m on sabbatical. I don’t care what color the grass is or even what side it’s on.

THIRTEEN
